title: q5 — The $1 land deal: Surplus Land Act, gift clause, and the local preference
area: Surplus Land Act / gift of public funds / fair housing
facts:
The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.
question: Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?
why_contested: Each theory is independently debatable — the SLA exemption thresholds and covenant-length requirements are technical and HCD's interpretations are aggressive but not court-tested; gift-clause doctrine gives broad deference to recited public purposes but a $11.8M discount to a for-profit developer strains it; local preferences are common political currency yet repeatedly flagged as disparate-impact risks in demographically unrepresentative cities. The interaction (restructuring to fix one exposure can worsen another) is where the real difficulty lives.
proposition: If challenged as structured, the disposition would be held unlawful under the Surplus Land Act (i.e., HCD's position that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required process was violated would substantially prevail).
